  method            from
  as.zoo.data.frame zoo 



#| cache: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false
gauges <- tribble(
  ~site_no,     ~name,                            ~state,
  "06752260",   "Poudre at Mouth",                  "CO"
)

poudre_flow <- readNWISdv(
  siteNumber = "06752260",
  parameterCd = "00060",
  startDate   = "2013-01-01",
  endDate     = "2023-12-31"
) |>
  renameNWISColumns() |>
  mutate(Date = yearmonth(Date)) |>
  group_by(Date) |>
  summarise(Flow = mean(Flow))

Warning in readNWISdv(siteNumber = "06752260", parameterCd = "00060", startDate
= "2013-01-01", : NWIS servers are slated for decommission. Please begin to
migrate to read_waterdata_daily.


GET: https://waterservices.usgs.gov/nwis/dv/?site=06752260&format=waterml%2C1.1&ParameterCd=00060&StatCd=00003&startDT=2013-01-01&endDT=2023-12-31

glimpse(raw)

function (length = 0L)  



basin <- findNLDI(nwis = "06752260", find = "basin")

sdate <- as.Date("2013-01-01")
edate <- as.Date("2023-12-31")

gm <- getTerraClim(
  AOI       = basin$basin,
  var       = c("tmax", "ppt", "srad"),
  startDate = sdate,
  endDate   = edate
) |>
  unlist() |>
  rast() |>
  exact_extract(basin$basin, "mean", progress = FALSE)

Warning: package 'future' was built under R version 4.5.2


Warning in .local(x, y, ...): Polygons transformed to raster CRS. To avoid
on-the-fly coordinate transformation, ensure that st_crs() returns the same
result for both raster and polygon inputs.

historic <- mutate(gm, id = "gridmet") |>
  pivot_longer(cols = -id) |>
  mutate(name = sub("^mean\\.", "", name)) |>
  tidyr::extract(name, into = c("var", "index"), "(.*)_([^_]+$)") |>
  mutate(index = as.integer(index)) |>
  mutate(Date = yearmonth(seq.Date(sdate, edate, by = "month")[as.numeric(index)])) |>
  pivot_wider(id_cols = Date, names_from = var, values_from = value) |>
  right_join(poudre_flow, by = "Date")

# Validate: should have 132 rows (11 years × 12 months). If fewer, climate data
# is missing for some months and those flow months were silently dropped.
stopifnot("Climate join dropped flow months — check GridMET download" = nrow(historic) == 132)


sdate <- as.Date("2024-01-01")
edate <- as.Date("2033-12-31")

maca <- getMACA(
  AOI      = basin$basin,
  var      = c("tasmax", "pr", "rsds"),
  timeRes  = "month",
  startDate = sdate,
  endDate   = edate
) |>
  unlist() |>
  rast() |>
  exact_extract(basin$basin, "mean", progress = FALSE)

Multiple ensembles available per model. Since `ensemble = NULL`, we default to:
    > CCSM4 [rcp45] [r6i1p1]
    > CCSM4 [rcp45] [r6i1p1]
    > CCSM4 [rcp45] [r6i1p1]


Warning in .local(x, y, ...): Polygons transformed to raster CRS. To avoid
on-the-fly coordinate transformation, ensure that st_crs() returns the same
result for both raster and polygon inputs.

future <- mutate(maca, id = "maca") |>
  pivot_longer(cols = -id) |>
  mutate(name = sub("^mean\\.", "", name)) |>
  tidyr::extract(name, into = c("var", "index"), "(.*)_([^_]+$)") |>
  mutate(index = as.integer(index)) |>
  mutate(Date = yearmonth(seq.Date(sdate, edate, by = "month")[as.numeric(index)])) |>
  pivot_wider(id_cols = Date, names_from = var, values_from = value)

# Rename the 3 climate columns to match GridMET names (Date column stays first)
stopifnot("Unexpected MACA column count" = ncol(future) == 4)
names(future)[2:4] <- c("ppt", "srad", "tmax")  # order matches getMACA var request
future <- mutate(future, tmax = tmax - 273.15)   # Kelvin → °C

title: Lab 6
text:
Q2

# Static
ggplot(historic_ts, aes(x = Date, y = Flow)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Monthly mean flow (cfs)",
       title = "Cache la Poudre at Mouth — monthly mean Q") +
  theme_minimal()







# Interactive
plot_ly(historic_ts, x = ~as.Date(Date), y = ~Flow,
        type = "scatter", mode = "lines")

title: Lab 6
text:
Q3

library(feasts)

# Subseries plot
historic_ts |> gg_subseries(Flow) +
  labs(y = "Flow (cfs)", title = "Subseries: one panel per month")

Warning: `gg_subseries()` was deprecated in feasts 0.4.2.
ℹ Please use `ggtime::gg_subseries()` instead.
ℹ Graphics functions have been moved to the {ggtime} package. Please use
  `library(ggtime)` instead.








# Seasonal plot
historic_ts |> gg_season(Flow, labels = "right") +
  labs(y = "Flow (cfs)", title = "Seasonal: one line per year")

Warning: `gg_season()` was deprecated in feasts 0.4.2.
ℹ Please use `ggtime::gg_season()` instead.
ℹ Graphics functions have been moved to the {ggtime} package. Please use
  `library(ggtime)` instead.









Seasons are defined by seasonality by the index frequency. The tsibble is 1M with an implicit annual cycle. So each season is one calendar month and the cycle length is 12. This is mechanical rather than physical. The prompt feasts doesn’t know may-june is typically when snow melts it just knows that month 5 and month 6 are the 5th and 6th place of 12. For the seasonal shape there is a unimodal annual cycle which peaks from May-June with the rising limb starting in April and receding in July back to base flow in October. The timing of the peak is relatively stable from year to year but the amplitude varies a lot.There is two parts to the physical processes of the May-June peak. First, snowmelt is the main driver from the surrounding mountains. The secondary process is the hydrograph is shaped by operations which are timed to agricultural needs and demands

title: Lab 6
text:
Q4

# Lag plot on log1p so peak months don't crush the rest of the data
historic_ts |>
  mutate(log_flow = log1p(Flow)) |>
  gg_lag(log_flow, geom = "point", lags = c(1, 3, 6, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Lag plot of log1p(Flow)",
       x = "Lagged log_flow",
       y = "log_flow")

Warning: `gg_lag()` was deprecated in feasts 0.4.2.
ℹ Please use `ggtime::gg_lag()` instead.
ℹ Graphics functions have been moved to the {ggtime} package. Please use
  `library(ggtime)` instead.








# Formal tests — both, because they have opposite nulls
adf.test(historic_ts$Flow)    # H0: unit root (non-stationary)

Warning in adf.test(historic_ts$Flow): p-value smaller than printed p-value



    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  historic_ts$Flow
Dickey-Fuller = -6.2664, Lag order = 5, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary

kpss.test(historic_ts$Flow)   # H0: stationary


    KPSS Test for Level Stationarity

data:  historic_ts$Flow
KPSS Level = 0.35701, Truncation lag parameter = 4, p-value = 0.09568

# feasts equivalents — these are more useful for monthly data
historic_ts |> features(Flow, unitroot_kpss)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  kpss_stat kpss_pvalue
      <dbl>       <dbl>
1     0.357      0.0957

historic_ts |> features(Flow, unitroot_ndiffs)

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  ndiffs
   <int>
1      0

historic_ts |> features(Flow, unitroot_nsdiffs)   # seasonal differencing

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  nsdiffs
    <int>
1       1


What do the tests tell you? If the series is non-stationary, ARIMA will need d ≥ 1 (differencing). Does the lag plot support the same conclusion?

title: Lab 6
text:
Q5

dcmp <- historic_ts |>
  model(STL(Flow ~ trend(window = 21) +
                   season(window = "periodic"),
            robust = TRUE))

components(dcmp) |> autoplot()

Warning: `autoplot.dcmp_ts()` was deprecated in fabletools 0.6.0.
ℹ Please use `ggtime::autoplot.dcmp_ts()` instead.
ℹ Graphics functions have been moved to the {ggtime} package. Please use
  `library(ggtime)` instead.










historic_tbl <- historic_ts |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(date = as.Date(Date),
         log_flow = log1p(Flow)) |>
  select(date, tmax, ppt, srad, Flow, log_flow)

future_tbl <- future |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(date = as.Date(Date)) |>
  select(date, tmax, ppt, srad)

splits <- time_series_split(historic_tbl, assess = "24 months", cumulative = TRUE)

Using date_var: date



mods <- list(
  arima         = arima_reg()                                |> set_engine("auto_arima"),
  arima_boost   = arima_boost(min_n = 2, learn_rate = 0.015) |> set_engine("auto_arima_xgboost"),
  prophet       = prophet_reg()                              |> set_engine("prophet"),
  prophet_boost = prophet_boost(learn_rate = 0.1)            |> set_engine("prophet_xgboost"),
  ets           = exp_smoothing()                            |> set_engine("ets")
)


models_fit <- map(mods, ~ fit(.x, log_flow ~ .,
                              data = select(training(splits), -Flow)))

frequency = 12 observations per 1 year
frequency = 12 observations per 1 year


Disabling weekly seasonality. Run prophet with weekly.seasonality=TRUE to override this.


Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.


Disabling weekly seasonality. Run prophet with weekly.seasonality=TRUE to override this.


Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.


frequency = 12 observations per 1 year

models_tbl <- as_modeltime_table(models_fit)


calib_tbl <- models_tbl |>
  modeltime_calibrate(new_data = select(testing(splits), -Flow))

calib_tbl |>
  modeltime_accuracy() |>
  arrange(mase)

# A tibble: 5 × 9
  .model_id .model_desc                .type   mae  mape  mase smape  rmse   rsq
      <int> <chr>                      <chr> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1         2 ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] W… Test  0.469  14.5 0.476  12.9 0.595 0.831
2         5 ETS(A,N,A)                 Test  0.511  15.7 0.518  14.0 0.621 0.820
3         1 REGRESSION WITH ARIMA(3,1… Test  0.581  15.4 0.589  15.5 0.684 0.773
4         3 PROPHET W/ REGRESSORS      Test  0.581  16.0 0.589  16.7 0.693 0.783
5         4 PROPHET W/ XGBOOST ERRORS  Test  0.645  17.1 0.654  17.8 0.737 0.777



forecast_tbl <- calib_tbl |>
  modeltime_forecast(
    new_data    = select(testing(splits), -Flow),
    actual_data = historic_tbl
  ) |>
  mutate(
    .value   = expm1(.value),
    .conf_lo = expm1(pmax(.conf_lo, 0)),
    .conf_hi = expm1(.conf_hi)
  )

forecast_tbl |> plot_modeltime_forecast()






refit_tbl <- calib_tbl |>
  modeltime_refit(data = historic_tbl)

future_forecast <- refit_tbl |>
  modeltime_forecast(
    new_data    = future_tbl,
    actual_data = historic_tbl
  ) |>
  mutate(
    .value   = expm1(.value),
    .conf_lo = expm1(pmax(.conf_lo, 0)),
    .conf_hi = expm1(.conf_hi)
  )

upstream_raw <- readNWISdv(
  siteNumber  = "06746095",
  parameterCd = "00060",
  startDate   = "2013-01-01",
  endDate     = "2023-12-31"
) |>
  renameNWISColumns()

Warning in readNWISdv(siteNumber = "06746095", parameterCd = "00060", startDate
= "2013-01-01", : NWIS servers are slated for decommission. Please begin to
migrate to read_waterdata_daily.


GET: https://waterservices.usgs.gov/nwis/dv/?site=06746095&format=waterml%2C1.1&ParameterCd=00060&StatCd=00003&startDT=2013-01-01&endDT=2023-12-31

names(upstream_raw) 

[1] "agency_cd" "site_no"   "Date"      "Flow"      "Flow_cd"  

upstream <- upstream_raw |>
  mutate(date = floor_date(Date, "month")) |>     # replace `Date` with the actual name
  group_by(date) |>
  summarise(upstream_flow = mean(Flow, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Lab calls this pf_tbl — that's historic_tbl in our pipeline
pf_reg <- historic_tbl |>
  left_join(upstream, by = "date") |>
  drop_na(upstream_flow)

# Sanity check — how many months got dropped?
nrow(historic_tbl) - nrow(pf_reg)

[1] 2



splits_reg <- time_series_split(pf_reg, assess = "24 months", cumulative = TRUE)

Using date_var: date

models_reg <- map(mods, ~ fit(.x, log_flow ~ .,
                              data = select(training(splits_reg), -Flow)))

frequency = 12 observations per 1 year

models_reg_tbl <- as_modeltime_table(models_reg)

calib_reg_tbl <- models_reg_tbl |>
  modeltime_calibrate(new_data = select(testing(splits_reg), -Flow))

# Side-by-side MASE comparison
acc_base <- calib_tbl |> modeltime_accuracy() |>
  select(.model_desc, mase) |> rename(mase_climate = mase)

acc_reg <- calib_reg_tbl |> modeltime_accuracy() |>
  select(.model_desc, mase) |> rename(mase_with_upstream = mase)

left_join(acc_base, acc_reg, by = ".model_desc") |>
  mutate(delta = mase_climate - mase_with_upstream) |>
  arrange(desc(delta))

Describe what you see:
What does the trend tell you about long-term changes in Poudre flow? Is there evidence of a decline consistent with climate change or upstream demand? What drives the seasonal component? (Hint: snowmelt, irrigation withdrawals, reservoir releases) Are there any spikes in the remainder that correspond to anomalous years you can identify?
Examine your 10-year forecast and reflect on the following:
Model agreement: Do ARIMA and Prophet project similar trajectories? If they diverge, what does that tell you about their different assumptions (statistical stationarity vs. flexible trend)?
Forecast quality: Which model would you recommend to CWCB for water-supply planning? Justify with your accuracy metrics.
Negative predictions: If any model predicts negative flow, explain why this happens and what you would do to fix it in a production context (hint: log-transformation of Flow before modeling).
Regressor value: The MACA climate projections assume a specific emissions scenario. How does uncertainty in those projections propagate into your streamflow forecast?
Improvement path: What information not in tmax, ppt, srad would most improve this forecast? (Consider: snowpack, upstream storage, irrigation demand.)
